titles.title: Regent of England (rector regis et regni)

citations.title: Dictionary of National Biography, 1885-1900/Marshal, William (d.1219)
citations.quote: Marshal was formally chosen by the common consent to be 'rector regis et regni'

summary: William Marshal rose from a landless younger son of a minor Anglo-Norman baron to become one of the most powerful magnates in England, serving Henry II, Richard I, John, and Henry III across more than four decades. His 1189 marriage to Isabel de Clare, heiress of Pembroke and Leinster, made him one of the richest men in the realm. Named among King John's counsellors in the preamble of Magna Carta in 1215, he was chosen regent and protector of the boy king Henry III on John's death in 1216, led the royalist victory at the Battle of Lincoln in 1217, and reissued Magna Carta to secure the peace that ended the First Barons' War.

citations.title: William Marshal, 1st Earl of Pembroke
citations.quote: William Marshal, 1st Earl of Pembroke (1146 or 1147 – 14 May 1219)

citations.title: Dictionary of National Biography, 1885-1900/Marshal, William (d.1219)
citations.quote: Marshal was formally chosen by the common consent to be 'rector regis et regni'

uncertain: Sources disagree on William Marshal's exact birth year: Wikipedia gives '1146 or 1147', while the Dictionary of National Biography argues 1146 is more likely despite Marshal's own reported claim to be over eighty years old in 1216.

name: John Marshal

citations.title: John Marshal (died 1165)
citations.quote: John Marshal died on 22 July 1165

citations.title: Patrick, 1st Earl of Salisbury
citations.quote: That year, Patrick married his sister, Sibyl, to John fitzGilbert the Marshal, who had been a local rival of his, and transferred his allegiance from King Stephen to the Empress Matilda.

citations.title: John Marshal (died 1165)
citations.quote: He later married "Sybil, sister of Patrick of Salisbury" after being forced to annul his first marriage on consanguinity grounds.

citations.title: John Marshal (died 1165)
citations.quote: He served as marshal of England, inheriting the position from his father Gilbert Giffard, who held it under King Henry I.

summary: John Marshal was a minor Anglo-Norman baron who served as marshal of England and fought for the Empress Matilda during the civil war of Stephen's reign known as the Anarchy. When King Stephen besieged him at Newbury Castle in 1152, John surrendered his young son William as a hostage to secure a truce but had no intention of honouring its terms; according to William's biographer, when Stephen threatened to hang the boy, John replied that he still had the means to father more sons. Stephen spared William. John died on 22 July 1165.

citations.title: Dictionary of National Biography, 1885-1900/Marshal, William (d.1219)
citations.quote: the young William was given as hostage for a truce and the surrender of the castle. John Marshal refused to keep the terms, and his son's life would have been sacrificed had not Stephen...protected him.

citations.title: John Marshal (died 1165)
citations.quote: Though Stephen threatened to execute the boy, he ultimately took pity on William, keeping him at court until after the civil war ended in 1153.

uncertain: The anecdote of John's 'hammer and anvil' reply to Stephen's threat to hang William is preserved by the Dictionary of National Biography as coming from 'William's biographer' — i.e. the History of William Marshal, a 13th-century verse biography commissioned by the family after his death — and is reported here as that source's account rather than settled fact.

name: Sibyl of Salisbury

citations.title: Patrick, 1st Earl of Salisbury
citations.quote: That year, Patrick married his sister, Sibyl, to John fitzGilbert the Marshal, who had been a local rival of his, and transferred his allegiance from King Stephen to the Empress Matilda.

summary: Sibyl was the sister of Patrick, Earl of Salisbury. In 1141 her brother married her to John FitzGilbert the Marshal, a local rival he was reconciling to the Empress Matilda's cause during the Anarchy. She was the mother of William Marshal, later Earl of Pembroke and regent of England.

citations.title: Dictionary of National Biography, 1885-1900/Marshal, William (d.1219)
citations.quote: second son of John Marshal (d. 1164?) [q. v.], by his second wife, Sibyl, sister of Patrick, earl of Salisbury.

citations.title: Patrick, 1st Earl of Salisbury
citations.quote: That year, Patrick married his sister, Sibyl, to John fitzGilbert the Marshal, who had been a local rival of his, and transferred his allegiance from King Stephen to the Empress Matilda.

uncertain: No reliable primary or reference source consulted gives Sibyl of Salisbury's birth or death date; both are omitted rather than guessed.

titles.title: 4th Countess of Pembroke and Striguil (suo jure)

citations.title: Isabel de Clare, 4th Countess of Pembroke
citations.quote: suo jure 4th Countess of Pembroke and Striguil

summary: Isabel de Clare, born about 1172 in Leinster, was the daughter and heiress of Richard 'Strongbow' de Clare and Aoife of Leinster. Her 1189 marriage to the household knight William Marshal transferred to him the vast de Clare lordships of Pembroke, Striguil, and Leinster, making him one of the greatest magnates in the realm. She bore William ten children and died on 11 March 1220 at Chepstow.

citations.title: Aoife of Leinster

citations.title: Richard de Clare, 2nd Earl of Pembroke
citations.quote: Justiciar of Ireland

citations.title: Richard de Clare, 2nd Earl of Pembroke
citations.quote: also Lord of Leinster and Justiciar of Ireland

summary: Richard de Clare, known as Strongbow, was the second Earl of Pembroke. Recruited by the deposed King of Leinster, Diarmait Mac Murchada, to help retake his kingdom in exchange for marriage to Mac Murchada's daughter Aoife and succession to Leinster, Strongbow led Anglo-Norman forces that seized Waterford and Dublin in 1169-70. He married Aoife at Waterford in August 1170 and served as Justiciar of Ireland under Henry II until his death in Dublin in 1176. His daughter Isabel de Clare inherited his lordships and later married William Marshal.

citations.title: Richard de Clare, 2nd Earl of Pembroke
citations.quote: Dermot came to an agreement with Richard: If the latter helped the deposed king in the retaking of Leinster, he could have Aoife, Dermot's eldest daughter, in marriage, along with the succession to the crown if it was regained.

citations.title: Anglo-Norman invasion of Ireland
citations.quote: In 1170, there were further Norman landings, led by the Earl of Pembroke, Richard 'Strongbow' de Clare.

uncertain: Wikipedia's Richard de Clare article dates the marriage to Aoife to 'about 26 August 1171' at Reginald's Tower, while its Aoife of Leinster article dates it to 25 August 1170 at Christchurch Cathedral, Waterford, immediately after the fall of the city; this record follows the 1170/Christchurch Cathedral account, which aligns with the wider chronology of the 1169-71 invasion.

name: Aoife of Leinster
role: Countess of Pembroke; Lady of Leinster

citations.title: Aoife of Leinster
citations.quote: Aoife MacMurrough (Irish: Aoife Nic Murchada; c. 1153 – c. 1188), also known as Eva of Leinster or Red Eva, was an Irish noblewoman.

citations.title: Aoife of Leinster
citations.quote: Died c. 1188 (aged about 35) Monmouthshire, Wales

citations.title: Aoife of Leinster
citations.quote: Aoife married Richard almost immediately after on 25 August in Christchurch Cathedral in Waterford.

summary: Aoife MacMurrough, also called Eva of Leinster, was the daughter of Diarmait Mac Murchada, King of Leinster. Under an agreement between her father and the Anglo-Norman lord Richard 'Strongbow' de Clare, she married Strongbow at Waterford in August 1170, shortly after his forces captured the city during the invasion of Ireland. She bore two children, Gilbert and Isabel de Clare, and died around 1188, being buried at Tintern Abbey in Monmouthshire.

citations.title: Aoife of Leinster
citations.quote: Aoife MacMurrough was the daughter of Dermot MacMurrough, King of Leinster

citations.title: Aoife of Leinster
citations.quote: At some point between her marriage in 1170 and Richard's death in 1176, Aoife gave birth to two children: Gilbert (born c. 1173) and Isabel.

uncertain: Wikipedia's Richard de Clare article dates the marriage to 'about 26 August 1171' at Reginald's Tower, while the Aoife of Leinster article dates it to 25 August 1170 at Christchurch Cathedral, Waterford; this record follows the 1170 account, consistent with the chronology of Waterford's capture.

citations.title: William Marshal, 2nd Earl of Pembroke
citations.quote: William died on 6 April 1231

summary: William Marshal, eldest son of William Marshal and Isabel de Clare, was born in Normandy around 1190. He was one of the twenty-five baronial executors named to enforce Magna Carta in 1215 and fought alongside his father at the Battle of Lincoln in 1217. He succeeded as 2nd Earl of Pembroke in 1219, married Eleanor, sister of Henry III, in 1224, and died in 1231.

citations.quote: he succeeded as Earl of Pembroke on 27 November 1245 but "never received the inheritance" before his death approximately 26 days later

summary: Anselm Marshal, youngest son of William Marshal and Isabel de Clare, was said to have been born during his father's exile in Ireland when William was in his mid-sixties. He briefly succeeded as 6th Earl of Pembroke on the death of his brother Walter in November 1245 but died himself only weeks later, on 23 December 1245, without formally receiving the inheritance, ending the male Marshal line.

citations.title: Anselm Marshal, 6th Earl of Pembroke

uncertain: No firm birth date is given in the source consulted; it is described only as during his father's Irish exile when William was in his mid-sixties, which is itself imprecise.

summary: Maud (Matilda) Marshal, eldest daughter of William Marshal and Isabel de Clare, was born by 1192 at the latest. She married Hugh Bigod, 3rd Earl of Norfolk, before Lent 1207, and after his death married William de Warenne, 5th Earl of Surrey, in 1225. She died on 27 March 1248.

summary: Isabel Marshal, daughter of William Marshal and Isabel de Clare, was born on 9 October 1200. She married Gilbert de Clare, Earl of Gloucester and Hertford, in 1217, and after his death in 1230 married Richard, Earl of Cornwall, brother of Henry III, in March 1231. She died on 17 January 1240 at Berkhamsted Castle.
